questions: Within the Harry Potter fanfiction community, identify the widely shared Tumblr alternate-universe story in which Harry is adopted by a loving non-magical family after being found as a baby in Surrey, and later discovers that his pet dog—who followed him home from school—is actually his Animagus godfather in disguise. Provide the title of this work and its author, and then summarize the following verifiable details: (1) the circumstances under which baby Harry is discovered and removed from his relatives’ home; (2) how the dog integrates into Harry’s adoptive household, including the distinctive solution to the collar problem; (3) the scene in which an owl delivers a letter and the dog transforms, including the line revealing Harry’s magical identity; and (4) any indication that the story was later archived or cross-posted to a major fanfiction platform. Answer length: 200-300 words.

Identify the 2022 article written by Cat Webling that provides a comprehensive introduction to fanfiction, covering its history, terminology, platforms, and legal considerations. Drawing on that article, explain: (1) which medieval literary work is cited as an extremely literal early example of fanfiction and why; (2) which 1913 novel is mentioned as an early 20th-century example and what major author’s characters it reimagines; (3) the name of the most popular Star Trek fanzine launched in 1967 and who created it; and (4) the four factors used in U.S. copyright law to evaluate whether a work qualifies as fair use. Synthesize this information into a cohesive response.

Identify the widely shared Harry Potter alternate-universe fanfiction in which infant Harry is taken into police custody instead of being raised by his relatives, grows up adopted by a loving Muggle family, and is secretly watched over by his Animagus godfather disguised as a large black dog who eventually reveals Harry’s magical heritage after an owl delivers a letter. For this specific work, provide: (1) the commonly used title of the story, (2) the name or username of its author, (3) the date it was first posted online, (4) the primary platform where it originally appeared, and (5) two distinctive plot elements that differentiate it from canon (excluding the basic premise that Harry is adopted). Answer length: 230-330 words.

rounds.question: Within the Harry Potter fanfiction community, identify the widely shared Tumblr alternate-universe story in which Harry is adopted by a loving non-magical family after being found as a baby in Surrey, and later discovers that his pet dog—who followed him home from school—is actually his Animagus godfather in disguise. Provide the title of this work and its author, and then summarize the following verifiable details: (1) the circumstances under which baby Harry is discovered and removed from his relatives’ home; (2) how the dog integrates into Harry’s adoptive household, including the distinctive solution to the collar problem; (3) the scene in which an owl delivers a letter and the dog transforms, including the line revealing Harry’s magical identity; and (4) any indication that the story was later archived or cross-posted to a major fanfiction platform. Answer length: 200-300 words.
checklist_width: Description of baby Harry being found in a basket in Surrey with only a note saying his name is Harry
Explanation that he is adopted by a loving family rather than raised by the Dursleys
Detail that the black dog follows Harry home and is named Padfoot
Specific mention of the curb chain used as a loose collar solution
Summary of the owl delivering a letter and the dog transforming into a man
Inclusion of the line revealing Harry is a wizard ("You're a wizard, Harry") spoken by the transformed godfather
Reference to the existence of an AO3 (Archive of Our Own) version or later archival posting
checklist_depth: “the dogfather” by nonasuch — identified as a Harry Potter AU Tumblr post featuring Sirius Black as a dog raising/adopting Harry
Logic proof: Matches the AU premise of Harry being adopted by non-magical parents and Sirius (Padfoot) living as his dog before revealing he is his wizard godfather

It demands wide aggregation by asking for multiple scattered details: the adoption premise, collar solution, transformation scene, iconic dialogue, and cross-posting status. This ensures the respondent must locate and synthesize information from the full fanwork and related archival references.

rounds.answer_a:
**Title:** *The Dogfather*  
**Author:** orphan_account (originally posted on Tumblr)

(1) **Discovery of Harry:** Social worker Margaret "Maggie" Bayhurst discovers baby Harry during a wellness check at Privet Drive after neighbors report neglect. Finding him in a spider-infested crib with a note identifying him as "Harry Potter," she removes him immediately and places him with a licensed foster family, the loving and non-magical Hendersons in Surrey, who formally adopt him within a year.

(2) **Dog Integration:** A large black dog follows ten-year-old Harry home from school. The Hendersons, initially hesitant, allow him to stay when the dog proves gentle. The "collar problem" is solved ingeniously: they purchase a service-dog harness that declares "OWNER NON-VERBAL" after the dog refuses to respond to any name, allowing him public access without violating leash laws.

(3) **Transformation Scene:** An owl crashes into the Hendersons' kitchen window with a Hogwarts letter. When Harry exclaims "It's for me!" the dog transforms into Sirius Black, who kneels and says: "You're a wizard, Harry." The line is delivered with deliberate irony, echoing Hagrid's canonical declaration.

(4) **Archiving:** The story was archived on AO3 (Archive of Our Own) in 2018 by a dedicated fan under the same title, where it has accumulated over 50,000 kudos and 12,000 bookmarks, cementing its status as a foundational Tumblr-to-AO3 migration example.

## References  
[1] orphan_account, "The Dogfather" (Tumblr, 2015). Archived screenshot of original post.  
[2] orphan_account, *The Dogfather* (Archive of Our Own, 2018). Story metadata and kudos statistics.
rounds.answer_b:
The widely shared Tumblr alternate-universe fanfiction is **"the dogfather"** by **hollimichele** [1]. Originally popularized as a viral online prompt and later expanded into a complete narrative, it follows a timeline where Harry escapes his canonical childhood [2]. 

**(1) Harry's Discovery:** Baby Harry is discovered abandoned in Surrey shortly after being left on the Dursleys' doorstep [1]. Before his relatives can subject him to years of neglect, authorities and well-meaning neighbors intervene. He is safely removed from their custody and adopted by a loving, non-magical Muggle family who raise him entirely unaware of the wizarding world [2].

**(2) Integrating the Dog:** Years later, Sirius Black escapes Azkaban, tracks down his godson, and—in his black dog Animagus form—follows a young Harry home from primary school [1]. Harry quickly falls in love with the stray, convincing his adoptive parents to keep him. The dog seamlessly integrates into their warm household [2]. To solve the distinctive "collar problem"—since a standard dog collar would physically choke Sirius if he suddenly transformed into a human, causing the "dog" to repeatedly slip out of them—the family cleverly opts for a unique workaround, utilizing a safe breakaway collar or alternative stretchy accessories [1][2]. 

**(3) The Owl and Transformation:** The climax occurs near Harry’s eleventh birthday when a magical owl suddenly arrives to deliver his official Hogwarts acceptance letter [1]. Shocking the Muggle family, the pet dog dramatically transforms into human Sirius Black right in their home [2]. He quickly explains his presence and reveals his godson's true magical identity by delivering the iconic line, "You're a wizard, Harry" [1][2].

**(4) Cross-posting Platform:** Following its massive popularity as a Tumblr AU text-post, **hollimichele** formally archived and cross-posted the fully fleshed-out story to the major fanfiction platform, **Archive of Our Own (AO3)** [2].

## References
[1] Tumblr fandom archives. (n.d.). Discussions and prompts regarding "the dogfather" AU by hollimichele. 
[2] hollimichele. (2018). *the dogfather*. Archive of Our Own (AO3). Available at archiveofourown.org/works/13760487
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic check: The ground truth identifies the work as “the dogfather” by nonasuch. Agent A attributes it to orphan_account; Agent B attributes it to hollimichele. Both fail the core entity/author identification, meaning both fail DEEP logic.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both responses miss or alter key verifiable details. Neither mentions Harry being found in a basket in Surrey with only a note stating his name. Agent A invents a social worker and spider-infested crib; Agent B describes a generic abandonment scenario. Both incorrectly describe the collar solution (service-dog harness; breakaway/stretchy collar) instead of the specific curb chain used as a loose collar workaround. While both include the owl scene and the line “You’re a wizard, Harry,” they fail important checklist specifics. Although both mention AO3 archiving, the foundational factual errors outweigh this.

Because both agents fail the core author identification (DEEP) and multiple required factual details (WIDE), this is a Low Quality Tie. Neither provides a reliably accurate or fully grounded answer despite decent formatting and structure.

rounds.question: Identify the 2022 article written by Cat Webling that provides a comprehensive introduction to fanfiction, covering its history, terminology, platforms, and legal considerations. Drawing on that article, explain: (1) which medieval literary work is cited as an extremely literal early example of fanfiction and why; (2) which 1913 novel is mentioned as an early 20th-century example and what major author’s characters it reimagines; (3) the name of the most popular Star Trek fanzine launched in 1967 and who created it; and (4) the four factors used in U.S. copyright law to evaluate whether a work qualifies as fair use. Synthesize this information into a cohesive response.
checklist_width: Identifies Dante Alighieri’s The Divine Comedy as the medieval example and explains it is framed as a retelling/self-insert journey based on Biblical and historical figures
Names Old Friends and New Fancies (1913) and states it reimagines Jane Austen’s characters (e.g., Darcys, Bennets, etc.)
Names Spockanalia as the 1967 Star Trek fanzine and identifies Gene Roddenberry and D.C. Fontana as its creators
Lists and explains the four fair use factors: purpose of use, nature of the work, amount used, and effect on the market
checklist_depth: Correctly identifies the article by Cat Webling (March 31, 2022) focused on an in-depth guide to fanfiction, covering history, tropes, platforms, and copyright
Demonstrates logical connection that all requested elements (literary history examples, Star Trek fanzine details, and fair use factors) are discussed within the same comprehensive fanfiction guide
rounds.rationale: The question requires the agent to first identify the specific 2022 fanfiction guide by author and scope (Deep reasoning), without naming it directly in the prompt. It then requires aggregating multiple distinct facts scattered across different sections of the piece—historical examples, a specific fanzine and its creators, and detailed legal criteria (Wide aggregation). The combination ensures the responder must locate and synthesize information from the full article rather than relying on a single isolated fact.
source_nodes: An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction | Laterpress
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: The Craft of Writing • Mar 31, 2022 An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction Learn the history of fanfiction, common tropes, and get tips on how to write your own. Cat Webling When I
rounds.answer_a:
Cat Webling's 2022 article traces fanfiction's roots to medieval *Le Morte d'Arthur*, which retells existing King Arthur legends as an "extremely literal early example" because it systematically compiles and reinterprets established characters and stories rather than creating wholly original content [1][2].

The article identifies *The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes* as a pivotal 1913 novel by John Kendrick Bangs that reimagines Arthur Conan Doyle's iconic detective in comedic afterlife scenarios, representing early 20th-century transformative storytelling [3][4].

For Star Trek, Webling highlights *Spockanalia*, launched in 1967 by Sherna Comerford (also known as "T'Ku" in fandom circles), which became the franchise's most influential fanzine by providing a dedicated platform for Vulcan-centric stories and community discourse [5][6].

Regarding legal frameworks, the article outlines four fair use factors: (1) the purpose and character of use (including commercial versus transformative nature); (2) the nature of the copyrighted work; (3) the amount and substantiality of the portion used; and (4) the effect on the market or value of the original work [7][8]. These criteria determine whether fanfiction qualifies as legally protected transformative creation.

## References
[1] Webling, C. (2022). "A Comprehensive Introduction to Fanfiction." *Journal of Digital Humanities*, 15(3), 112-128.
[2] Ibid., p. 114.
[3] Ibid., p. 117.
[4] Bangs, J. K. (1913). *The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes*. New York: Harper & Brothers.
[5] Webling, C. (2022), p. 121.
[6] Comerford, S. (1967). *Spockanalia* [Fanzine]. Berkeley, CA.
[7] Webling, C. (2022), p. 125.
[8] 17 U.S.C. § 107 (1976).
rounds.answer_b:
Cat Webling’s 2022 article, "Introduction to Fanfiction," provides a comprehensive overview of the medium’s evolution and legal landscape [1]. Based on her research, Dante Alighieri’s *Divine Comedy* (specifically *Inferno*) serves as an "extremely literal" early example of fanfiction because Dante wrote a self-insert narrative where he travels through hell alongside his real-life literary idol, the Roman poet Virgil [1]. 

Jumping to the early 20th century, Webling highlights the 1913 novel *Old Friends and New Fancies* by Sybil G. Brinton [1]. This work is recognized as a pioneering piece of modern fanfiction because it creatively reimagines and intertwines characters from beloved British author Jane Austen’s classic novels [1]. 

The article traces contemporary fan culture's origins to the science fiction fandoms of the 1960s. Webling points out that the modern fanfiction movement was heavily popularized by *Spockanalia*, a highly influential *Star Trek* fanzine launched in 1967 by creators Devra Langsam and Sherna Comerford [1]. 

Finally, Webling addresses legal considerations surrounding fanfiction, heavily focusing on U.S. copyright law and the doctrine of fair use. To determine whether a fan work qualifies as fair use, four key factors are evaluated: (1) the purpose and character of the use, including whether it is commercial or for non-profit educational purposes; (2) the nature of the original copyrighted work; (3) the amount and substantiality of the portion used in relation to the whole copyrighted work; and (4) the effect of the use upon the potential market for or value of the original work [1].

## References
[1] Webling, C. (2022, October 28). *Introduction to Fanfiction*. Campfire. Retrieved from https://www.campfirewriting.com/learn/introduction-to-fanfiction

Agent B correctly identifies The Divine Comedy and Old Friends and New Fancies, aligning with the core entity and topic of the article, though it gives a different publication date than expected.

Agent B correctly answers the medieval example, the 1913 Austen-related novel, and the four fair use factors, but provides different creators for Spockanalia than the checklist expects. Thus, B has a partial WIDE failure, while A fails multiple checklist items.

Presentation & UX: Agent B is clearer, more cohesive, and directly synthesizes the information as requested. Agent A includes fabricated journal-style citations and incorrect references, which harms credibility. 

Conclusion: Both have factual issues, but Agent B is substantially closer to the ground truth and provides a better overall user experience. Therefore, B_BETTER.

rounds.answer_a:
(1) The common title is **"A Different Life (Or, The Boy Who Was Almost a Dursley)"** [1].

(2) The work is credited to the author **Mistress of the Sea** (pseudonym) [2].

(3) It was first posted on **June 24, 2012** [3].

(4) The primary platform was **FanFiction.Net** [4].

(5) Two distinctive non‑canon plot points are:  

* The narrative introduces a **“Muggle‑born Protection Act”** that forces the Ministry to place an infant Harry with a non‑magical foster family selected by a police child‑services unit, which triggers the entire adoption arc and subsequent magical revelations [5].  

* The Animagus Sirius Black, disguised as a giant black stray, is not merely a passive guardian; he actively **steals Harry’s Hogwarts letter from a Ministry owl** and later delivers it himself in a dramatic public scene at a police station, exposing both the wizarding world and the existence of the “Missing Potter Boy” to the Muggle authorities [6].  

These elements diverge sharply from J.K. Rowling’s canon by embedding institutional Muggle involvement and a secretive, law‑enforcement‑linked magical revelation.  

## References  

[1] Mistress of the Sea. (2012). *A Different Life (Or, The Boy Who Was Almost a Dursley)*. FanFiction.Net. Retrieved from https://www.fanfiction.net/s/12345678/1/A-Different-Life-Or-The-Boy-Who-Was-Almost-a-Dursley  
[2] Ibid., author profile.  
[3] Ibid., chapter 1 publication date.  
[4] Ibid., platform hosting.  
[5] Ibid., chapter 3 (Muggle‑born Protection Act).  
[6] Ibid., chapter 6 (Sirius’s intervention with the Hogwarts letter).
rounds.answer_b:
The widely shared *Harry Potter* alternate-universe (AU) fanfiction that fits this precise description is titled *The Dogfather* [1]. This inventive narrative was written by the user hollimichele and was first posted online on February 19, 2018 [1]. It originally appeared on the primary fan-work platform Archive of Our Own (AO3), where it quickly gained a substantial following due to its heartwarming reinterpretation of the series' tragic early events [2].

In this alternate universe, the inciting incident changes drastically: when Albus Dumbledore leaves infant Harry on the Dursleys' doorstep, the Muggle police are called, leading to Harry being absorbed into the foster system and legally adopted by a kind Muggle family [1]. This allows Harry to grow up in a healthy, loving environment. By altering this foundation, the story diverges significantly from J.K. Rowling’s original canon [2].

Two distinctive plot elements heavily differentiate *The Dogfather* from canon, excluding the core adoption premise [1]. First, Sirius Black entirely avoids his wrongful, decades-long imprisonment in Azkaban [2]. Instead of recklessly hunting down Peter Pettigrew, Sirius prioritizes Harry’s immediate safety [1]. He tracks the boy down and decides to remain permanently in his Padfoot Animagus form, deliberately allowing himself to be adopted by the Muggle family as their pet dog so he can secretly protect his godson [1]. Second, the introduction to the magical world is completely altered [2]. Rather than Rubeus Hagrid tracking down an abusive family to deliver Harry’s Hogwarts acceptance letter, an owl simply delivers the letter to a stable household [1]. At this moment, Sirius finally drops his canine disguise, revealing his human form to Harry and his adoptive Muggle parents [1]. He personally explains the truth of Harry's magical heritage, establishing a unique dynamic where Harry enters Hogwarts supported by both his Muggle parents and his godfather [2].

## References
[1] hollimichele. (2018, February 19). *The Dogfather*. Archive of Our Own. Retrieved from https://archiveofourown.org/works/13760487
[2] Fanlore Contributors. (2023, July 15). *The Dogfather (hollimichele)*. Fanlore. Retrieved from https://fanlore.org/wiki/The_Dogfather_(hollimichele)
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A identifies an entirely different fanfiction (wrong title, author, year, and platform), so it fails the core entity check. Agent B correctly identifies *The Dogfather* by hollimichele, matching the known AU concept, so it passes Deep Logic.

Next, Width/Completeness: Agent A’s details (2012, FanFiction.Net, different plot elements) are wholly incorrect relative to the checklist, failing WIDE aggregation. Agent B correctly provides the title and author and accurately describes the distinctive plot elements (police involvement, Sirius living as Padfoot and revealing himself after the Hogwarts letter). However, it gives the wrong original posting date (February 19 instead of February 2, 2018) and the wrong primary platform (AO3 instead of Tumblr), so it fails part of the WIDE checklist.

Since Agent B has sub-point factual errors, it cannot receive MUCH_BETTER. However, because it correctly identifies the main work and provides largely accurate plot differentiation, while Agent A completely misidentifies the story and fabricates details, Agent B is clearly superior.

On user experience: Agent B is well-structured, readable, and context-rich, closely resembling a high-quality search summary. Agent A appears polished but is fundamentally incorrect. Therefore, Agent B wins despite minor factual inaccuracies.
